response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "I've actually been curious about this topic because even though I don't spend much time on social media myself, my friends do and they often share misleading information. It's frustrating when you see something that looks viral but turns out to be false or exaggerated. From what I understand, fake content can range from harmless pranks to dangerous misinformation that affects people's health and safety.\n\nTo deal with this effectively, first you need to verify the sources before believing anything they post. Sometimes it seems like everyone shares without checking facts. Another thing is being aware of algorithms—they often prioritize engagement over accuracy, which means false content gets more attention. That's why fact-checking sites are really useful tools; I've recommended them to my friends whenever there's a questionable story.\n\nIt also helps if you focus on official accounts rather than random ones claiming expertise without credentials visible somewhere. And reporting mechanisms should be easy-to-use so people can flag misleading stuff quickly without too much hassle.",
  "reasoning_summary": "Based on the persona provided (young adult male, full-time student in Los Angeles with limited social media use but knowing about friends' experiences), I considered how everyday interactions might reveal insights into handling misinformation online. Since direct experience is lacking but relevant, drawing from common knowledge among peers seemed appropriate to maintain realism while avoiding technical jargon.",
  "tone": "Relatable yet informative",
  "citations": ["Various fact-checking organizations like Snopes"],
  "safety_notes": "None"
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've actually been curious about this topic because even though I don't spend much time on social media myself, my friends do and they often share misleading information. It's frustrating when you see something that looks viral but turns out to be false or exaggerated. From what I understand, fake content can range from harmless pranks to dangerous misinformation that affects people's health and safety.

To deal with this effectively, first you need to verify the sources before believing anything they post. Sometimes it seems like everyone shares without checking facts. Another thing is being aware of algorithms—they often prioritize engagement over accuracy, which means false content gets more attention. That's why fact-checking sites are really useful tools; I've recommended them to my friends whenever there's a questionable story.

It also helps if you focus on official accounts rather than random ones claiming expertise without credentials visible somewhere. And reporting mechanisms should be easy-to-use so people can flag misleading stuff quickly without too much hassle.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Based on the persona provided (young adult male, full-time student in Los Angeles with limited social media use but knowing about friends' experiences), I considered how everyday interactions might reveal insights into handling misinformation online. Since direct experience is lacking but relevant, drawing from common knowledge among peers seemed appropriate to maintain realism while avoiding technical jargon.
